mappings.rationaleAI: The scientific study of past life through fossils (B.1), encompassing the fossil record from Precambrian to Phanerozoic (B.2), mass extinction and background extinction events (B.3), and paleontological criteria for rock correlation including index fossils and assemblages (B.4). Biostratigraphy calibrates the geologic time scale.

4.54-billion-year history providing the context for layered rocks and depositional environments (A), the fossil record across all eons (B.2), and the absolute geologic time scale integrating stratigraphic, radiometric, and fossil data (D.4.a).

Spontaneous nuclear transformation of unstable isotopes, directly relevant to radiometric dating (D.2) including radioactivity and radioactive decay principles (D.2.a), dating methods (D.2.d), half-life characterization (D.2.b), and sources of error (D.2.c).

Mass extinctions punctuate the fossil record and ongoing human-driven extinction constitutes a sixth mass event (B.3), directly relevant to the appearance and disappearance of species revealed in the fossil record.

The three fundamental rock classes — igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic — constitute the layered rocks of the Earth's crust (A.1), including clastic sedimentary rocks (A.1.a), carbonate rocks (A.1.b), and volcanic rocks (A.1.c).

Spans the fossil record from Precambrian microbial evidence (B.2.a) through Phanerozoic diversification (B.2.b), including the Great Oxygenation Event, Cambrian explosion, and successive mass extinctions (B.3).

Dinosaurs are a major component of the Phanerozoic fossil record, specifically fossil reptiles (B.2.b.x), dominating terrestrial ecosystems from the Triassic through the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction.
Mollusca includes fossil mollusks such as ammonoids (B.2.b.v), with a fossil record extending from the Cambrian and significant stratigraphic utility.
Fish first appeared in the Cambrian with the Devonian as the 'Age of Fishes,' relevant to fossil fish in the Phanerozoic record (B.2.b.x).

Geology as the science of Earth's composition and change over time underpins stratigraphic interpretation (A), relative age dating and correlation (C), and absolute dating including geochemical methods (D).
Vertebrate fossil record spans from Cambrian jawless forms through Ordovician-Silurian jawed vertebrates, Devonian tetrapods, Triassic mammals and dinosaurs, and Jurassic birds (B.2.b.x).
The origin of life from non-living chemistry relates directly to the origin of life as documented in the Precambrian fossil record (B.2.a.i).
Birds as surviving theropod dinosaurs, with Archaeopteryx as a key Late Jurassic fossil, relevant to fossil birds in the Phanerozoic record (B.2.b.x).
Mammals originated from cynodont therapsids in the Late Triassic and diversified after the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction, relevant to fossil mammals (B.2.b.x).
Reptiles include fossil lineages diverging in the Permian, with the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction decimating non-avian dinosaurs, relevant to fossil reptiles (B.2.b.x).
Carbon-14 enables radiocarbon dating, a major radiometric dating method (D.2.d) for determining absolute ages of geological and archaeological materials.

Darwin's evolutionary theory established descent with modification as explanation for the fossil succession (A.2.c), providing the framework for understanding species appearance and disappearance in the fossil record (B.3).

The foundational unit of biodiversity, with species appearance and disappearance revealed in the fossil record (B.3) and species serving as the basis for paleontological correlation through index fossils and assemblages (B.4).
Crustaceans as mandibulate arthropods with fossil record extending to the Cambrian, relevant to fossil arthropods in the Phanerozoic record (B.2.b.ix).
The Pacific Ocean's geological history and seafloor features contribute to determination of rates of seafloor spreading (D.4.c).
The Iron Age's archaeological sequence connects relative dating to archaeological problems (C.2), marking the transition from prehistory to recorded history.
